title: Part 11
text: part of the earth’. The third, Further Observation on the Terrestrial Convulsions (April 1756), is a refutation of superstitious causes such as invidious astrological conjunctions. Against the theological speculation that the event naturally Liminality and the Modern 98 provoked, Kant insisted on secular explanations. Earthquakes are terrible, but they are accidents, not God’s punishment. As further evidence of how this period was a turning point for Kant, we simply can refer to the three events mentioned in the standard Kant chronology that took place between February and April, between the publication of his second and third essays on the earthquake. In March Kant submitted to the philosophy faculty his third Latin disputation, The Employment in Natural Philosophy of Metaphysics. On 8 April Kant applied for his first position as Professor. On 10 April the public defence of his Physical Monadology was celebrated; the first part of his third essay on the earthquake appeared that very same day. It is therefore not too much to say that some of Kant’s most decisive ideas – that would become foundational for the modern episteme – developed in conjunction with his reflections on this one particular liminal event. While the seismological quality of Kant’s observations remains a matter of debate for specialists, his evolutionary theory of the universe is still today seen as ‘the essence of modern models’ in cosmology (Coles as quoted in Schönfeld 2012). For Kant the question was: how can we recuperate trust in progress and science, and human cognition, in the face of this cataclysm? The Lisbon event inserted itself into a quite crucial debate running through the eighteenth century, concerning the divinity of the cosmos, and the role of good and evil in the world as we know it. In Theodicy, Leibniz had famously defined the universe as ‘the best of all possible worlds’. While nobody denied the existence of evil (as would fatally happen only in the twentieth century), evil was brought under the protective umbrella of a ‘greater good’. Things happened for a reason, and God’s will was good. Voltaire had initially aligned himself with Leibniz and the theological optimists, but the Lisbon earthquake completely broke his faith. Kant wanted to engage with the debates, and give them a positive answer. He needed to domesticate this apparently incomprehensible rupture and total destruction, of which the Lisbon earthquake was exemplary, in order to neutralize the threat such external events posed to the myth of progress grounded in human nature. The answer was given at two levels: first, by searching for natural laws behind the natural event. We see here a stern belief in technology’s promise to liberate man from the destructive power of nature. 1 Second, by reposing and re-founding 1 Kant (1756) forewords his first essay on the Earthquake thus: ‘Man, who is intrusted with the economy [Haushaltung] of the earth, [not only] possesses a capacity, [but] also takes pleasure in learning to know it, and through his insights praises the Creator. Even the terrible instruments of the visitation of the human species, the shakings of countries, the raging of the ocean that is [violently] agitated to its [very] bottom, the fire-spewing mountains, summon men to the contemplation of [nature], and are not less implanted in nature by God as a just consequence of constant laws than other usual causes of incommodity, which are held to be more natural only because we are better acquainted with them’. (Kant 2013, online version) Dimensions of Liminality 99 the question of human knowledge in the world. He would come to ‘cognitively manage’ the threat posed by the Lisbon earthquake in The Analytic of the Sublime of his 1790 Critique of Judgment. Kant never mentions the Lisbon earthquake in the Critique of Judgment, but it is there beneath the entire argument, as a ‘remembered anxiety’ (Ray 2004: 10). These influences on Kant’s thinking point with the utmost clarity towards the crucial links that exist between events, experience and thought in critical, liminal junctures. It is no minor point: it is often asserted that modern philosophy begins with Kant. Modernity is, also in this concrete sense, an institutionalized response to liminal experiences. As a natural event the Lisbon earthquake was a unique phenomenon, unprecedented and unrepeatable; as a large-scale liminal moment it had a widespread effect through the manifold reflections it provoked. In this cognitive and meaning-forming elaboration, it was transformed from a brutal fact to a complex cultural sign, redirecting material and mental processes in Europe and merging with the complex contemporary scientific and political developments that secured its long-term relevance. Kant’s elaboration of the earthquake also points to a central aspect of his entire thought, namely what Eric Voegelin identified as the clearly Gnostic elements in modern philosophy: the tendency to ground and organize thought and cognition in a heroic struggle against the chaos of the surrounding world. In this Gnostic enterprise, as we shall see in Chapter 5, he had been anticipated by Descartes and Hobbes. Kant’s reaction, and his whole philosophy, therefore exemplifies within the realm of thought what Weber identified as the inner-weltliche world-mastery as a path of salvation characteristic of the Judaeo-Christian tradition as it developed in the West. Liminality and its Complementary Concepts The analytical potential of liminality needs to be enhanced by other anthropological concepts, which to a surprising degree have been ignored by social theorists (Szakolczai 2009: 153; Horvath and Thomassen 2008). Three such concepts can be singled out: imitation, schismogenesis and trickster (logics). These complementary concepts become particularly relevant once we move into liminal situations without clearly defined boundaries of space and time, and without pre-defined masters of ceremony. They become necessary especially once we engage liminality in ‘mass’ societies, where ‘crowds’ or ‘publics’ constitute Turner’s communitas. Imitation, Repetition, Contagion: Plato, Kierkegaard, Tarde and Girard As is well-known, mimesis, the imitative aspect of human behaviour, was a central concern for Plato. Plato’s vision of rational (or balanced) 2 thought in classical 2 It is dutiful to remember that the word ‘rational’ stems from the Latin, ratio, and therefore etymologically resides with notions that may seem quite far from current usage, Liminality and the Modern 100 Greek philosophy was developed very much as a defence against the overwhelming powers of imitation that the Sophists purposefully perpetuated within his city. This concern prompted Plato to suggest a need for limits on mimetic endeavours – rules that could prevent or tame inessential ‘craft-making’. While this attempt to exclude uncontrolled mimetic practices and influences often has caused modern commentators to label Plato ‘anti-democratic’ and ‘totalitarian’ (apparently wishing for an elitist thought police led by philosopher-Kings like himself), it should rather be recognized that Plato was indeed putting his finger on a crucial problem, and one that is as pressing in today’s ‘democratic order’ as it was then. However, in modern philosophy, starting from Descartes, this crucial – and necessary – focus on imitation and imitative behaviour largely disappeared. Or rather, it was hidden away. The view slowly became established that only animals and children imitate, whilst mature adults, in the Kantian spirit, reach maturity and autonomy – the ‘modern self’, this unquestioned premise onto which the main branches of the social and political sciences soon started to build their string of axioms. The sad consequence is that even today, no introductory textbook in sociology lists imitation among the ‘key concepts’. The emphasis on imitation returned to modern philosophy via a series of figures who are only now starting to receive the attention they deserve, and which are rarely discussed together. In this context, one must recognize Søren Kierkegaard as a relatively unrecognized background figure of the social sciences. Repetition (gjentagelsen) was one of his key interests, to which he returned again and again. Imitation is, of course, nothing but a special case of repetition. Repetition, for Kierkegaard, is potentially a deeply meaningful act – although the specific meaning of the repetition depends on the context, and on its acting out, how it moves the soul, and how the soul engages with it. Kierkegaard wrote Gjentagelsen in 1843, with the subtitle, Et Forsøg i den experimenterende Psychologie. 3 The writing coincided with his four visits to Berlin between 1841 and 1846, during the very same period that Benjamin later characterized as the ‘great firsts’ of the modern (see Katz 1998: 1): the city was going through radical transformations set in motion by the forces of industrialization, gas-lighting, train-travel and photography. It cannot be a coincidence that Gjentagelsen is the one essay in which Kierkegaard describes a visit to Berlin; he does it by narrating his own attempt to re-live a pleasant episode of his previous visit, tragically failing to walk in his own foot-steps. Kierkegaard, in this same writing, also singled out the technique of photographing as an imitative act, also here anticipating Benjamin. Within sociology, no-one, of course, has analysed imitation better than Gabriel Tarde. Tarde argued that the tendency towards imitation is the single most fundamental drive behind the creation and development of social institutions namely as having to do with something or some act that has the right proportions, or is in balance or harmony. Strictly speaking, much of modern philosophical rationalism is not rational. 3 Which translates as ‘An experiment in experimental psychology’. Dimensions of Liminality 101 (1903). Durkheim ridiculed Tarde for this idea outright, but luckily it is now easy to see who was right and wrong. In his preface to the second edition of Laws of Imitation, Tarde sought to answer the critiques that had been raised in response to his first edition, namely that he was ‘overstretching’ the term, and that his focus on imitation led him towards ‘psychology’ and extreme individualism: But I have always given it [imitation] a very precise and characteristic meaning, that of the action at a distance of one mind upon the other, and of action that consists of a quasi-photographic reproduction of a cerebral image upon the sensitive plate of another brain. [...] By imitation I mean, every impression of an inter-psychical photography, so to speak, willed or not willed, passive or active. (Tarde 1903: xiv) These Tardean and Kierkegaardian themes 4 were also key concerns for Walter Benjamin. Besides his Small History of Photography, there are references to photography throughout his works. Much of his perhaps most famous essay, The Work of Art in an Age of Mechanical Reproduction, is devoted to film and photography. As Benjamin sees it, photography captures the very essence of the age of mechanical reproduction. In 1933, while still in Berlin, Benjamin worked on two essays, on the Doctrine of the Similar (2005a) and On the Mimetic Faculty (2005b). While Benjamin’s thought often lapsed into mysticism (and Marxism), he clearly perceived that from within Kantianism and Enlightenment philosophy, the very nature of mimesis cannot even be posed as a problem. And he somehow also understood that he lived in a dangerously mimetic age. It is therefore also no coincidence that the very same period was also the one in which Kierkegaard was receiving a peak of attention among German social thinkers. Adorno wrote his dissertation while Nazism was on the march. It was therefore important for him to lift the interpretation of Kierkegaard out of Heidegger’s analytical embrace. His dissertation was, symbolically, published the very same day that Hitler rose to power. Also in 1933, Karl Löwith (1897–1973), wrote two central essays on Kierkegaard and Nietzsche. The reading of Kierkegaard guided his diagnostic attempt to capture and come to terms with the present and overcome nihilism (he was forced to flee Germany that same year), and most clearly so in the essay, Kierkegaard und Nietzsche oder Philosophische und Teologische Uberwindung des Nihilismus. None of those themes were alien to Arnold van Gennep. Van Gennep never referred to the authors here mentioned (with the evident exception of Plato). However, his life-long ethnographic interest in coins and ‘marking’ or stamping techniques clearly relates to the issue. More directly still, it is striking to note how much emphasis he actually puts on the notion of ‘contagion’ in his classification 4 There is no indication that Tarde had read Kierkegaard. Yet, their critical analysis of communication and intersubjectivity in the ‘public’ (Tarde) or the ‘crowd’ (Kierkegaard 1964) share important affinities. Liminality and the Modern 102 and analysis of rites. The notion of ‘contagious rites’ is one of the subspecies recognized by van Gennep (1960: 7–14). Van Gennep noticed how many rites involve the belief that natural or acquired characteristics are material and transmissible, either through physical contact or over a distance (ibid.: 7). It is for this reason that neophytes, while in the liminal state, are in most cultural contexts considered dangerous and surrounded by a very explicit taboo prohibiting any physical contact between these liminars and people who stand outside the liminal. This is one of the aspects of rites of passage that is crucial to consider and further develop when applied to periods of transition in large-scale societies. The notion of contagion of course has strong links to the Tardean core notion of imitation: how we imitate, who we imitate and under what circumstances. As is well-known, those questions lie at the heart of the anthropology of René Girard, the main contemporary theorist of the mimetic aspect of human conduct. The starting point is the intuition, radically different from the perspective shared both by traditional associational psychology and Freudian approaches, that desire is primarily elicited by the object. According to Girard, quite on the contrary, desire is primarily mimetic: subject B desires an object, living or inanimate, in the sense of wanting to possess it fully and exclusively as it is already desired by subject A. B is only imitating A’s desire. The outcome of this ‘mimetic desire’, inevitably, is conflict, especially if desire has sexual aspects. This conflict leads to violence and can very easily escalate, imitatively, until it evolves into a full-scale civil war. Rituals and theatrical representations do not therefore simply imitate real acts, but are imitative of already imitative behaviour, and thus have a genuinely cathartic but also dangerous potential. In the last years of his life, Victor Turner came to recognize the theoretical importance of Girard (see for example Turner 1988: 34) and in the precise context of the ritual structure: crisis is contagious, and sometimes the ‘redressive machinery ... fails to function’ (ibid.: 35), leading to ‘a reversion to crisis’. Exploring what he would term ‘public liminality’, Turner also made increasing use of Bateson’s work on play and frame, and Bateson’s notion of ‘metacommunication’. In short, Turner became acutely aware of the imitative aspect of liminal situations, emphasizing that the middle phase of a ritual represented a mimetic enactment of a crisis, while Bateson similarly recognized the imitative, even mimic nature of the Naven ritual (Bateson 1958). In fact, as we shall argue below, his crucial notion of schismogenesis is nothing but a model of escalating imitative behaviour. Girard considered Turner and Bateson as close intellectual allies. According to Girard (1979), imitation plays a vital role in provoking and escalating violence. In fact, acts of violence, together with sexuality and laughter, belong to the most imitative aspects of human behaviour, where the performance of an act almost irresistibly leads to its repetitive imitation, until a certain kind of climax is reached. Girard’s work can here be combined with the perspective of van Gennep and Turner, as liminal situations are particularly conducive to mimesis and the escalation of violence; a phenomenon described by Girard as a ‘mimetic crisis’. Dimensions of Liminality 103 But how does such a system develop that legitimates and institutionalizes violence at the heart of social relations? Girard offers here his theory of scapegoating and the sacrificial mechanism. According to this theory, once the spiralling movement of escalating conflict and violence has started, there is only one possible solution: an innocent must be identified as guilty, excluded and killed, and in this way unanimity can be restored in society. In this sense, Girard sees sacrifice as the foundation of culture. There is every reason to reject this position as a general theory. 5 And yet it is certainly the case that sacrifice and the sacrificial mechanism can become a foundational element of cultural contexts, including perhaps ours the modern one. The general point to stress is this one: imitation poses particular problems in liminal situations, for in the absence of masters of ceremony, who should we follow, and who can take the lead out of the crisis? This takes us to the contrast between charismatic leaders and tricksters. Trickster Makes the World It must be recognized that he [Hitler] possesses a supreme dexterity in juxtaposing contradictions and imposing on his ‘subjects’ composite terms that, since they have no meaning, permit them to think anything. He reminds me of that celebrated ethnopsychologist Wilhelm Wundt, who, when he found it impossible to distinguish between Mythus (myth) and Märchen (folktales), invented the terms Mythusmärchen and its opposite, Märchenmythus, which denote neither the one nor the other yet somehow denote both simultaneously while also distinguishing between them without actually doing so. Hitler must certainly have read Wundt. None of which, however, diminishes his personal magnetism, increases the intelligence of his followers, or decreases our obligation to regard his movement very seriously, if not with dread. After which, perhaps, the Germans may find out what it means to be free. (Arnold van Gennep, in Mercure de France, as translated in Belmont) If rites of passage contain a chaotic, void-like liminal stage, then there must be something or somebody standing outside this void to guide people out of it. In anthropology, this figure is called the master of ceremonies. But what happens in liminal situations when there is no designated master of ceremonies? 5 Girard frequently refers to Mauss and Hertz’s 1898 essay on sacrifice; however, it was Mauss himself who came to realize the problematic aspect of this account which gave genealogical primacy to sacrifice. In The Gift, Mauss developed a much more elaborate theory of the foundations of social life, namely in gift-giving practices. This is a work that Girard does not really engage with. The point is that sacrifice is an institution that emerges right at the moment where a crisis escalates; but this ‘liminal moment’ clearly cannot be posited as the ‘beginning’ or foundation of culture, not even in an evolutionist sense. Liminality and the Modern 104 The short answer is that someone will invent himself as such, and present himself as possessing the key to law and order. He imitates, or highjacks the position of saviour. This is why the mythical figure of the trickster is a crucial complementary concept to the analysis of liminal situations in large-scale settings. Everything we have to say about this point obviously draws on the work of Agnes Horvath (1998), whose analysis of trickster mechanisms in political modernity has most recently been presented in her Modernism and Charisma (Horvath 2013). For a start, it is quite evident that the trickster has particular affinities with liminal situations. Discussing the liminal position and the didactics of the trickster figure in a Native American context 6 , Larry Ellis points out that ‘[the trickster’s] power is rooted in liminality and he calls it forth merely by expressing his liminal nature in the outlandish behavior for which he is so well known’ (Ellis 1993: 57). As Ellis says, the trickster is the ‘shaman of the liminal’. In the same vein, Joseph Campbell describes the Trickster as a ‘super-shaman ... The chief mythological character of the paleolithic world of story ... an epitome of the principle of disorder, he is nevertheless the culture bringer also’ (1959: 273). In liminality, as Turner described so vividly, the world is realized via reversals and opposites, inversions and reversals. This liminal setting is, alas, the most fertile terrain possible for tricksters to play their act. The ambivalent features of the trickster can be recognized at the start of any standard trickster tale or legend (see Radin 1972; Evans-Pritchard 1967; Hyde 1998). The trickster is a vagrant who happens to stumble into the village, appearing out of the blue. He tries to gain the confidence of villagers by telling tales and cracking jokes, thus by provoking laughter. He is an outsider who has no home and no existential commitments. He is also a mime. Under normal circumstances, tricksters are jokers that provoke laughter but cannot be taken seriously. In liminality this changes: as an outsider he might easily be perceived to represent a solution to a crisis. However, having no home, and therefore no real human and existential commitments, the trickster is not really interested in solving the liminal crisis: he simply pretends. In fact, being at home in liminality, or in homelessness, his real interest often lies in perpetuating such conditions of confusion and ambivalence. And in this he might succeed, as the title of Lewis Hyde’s book reminds us: Trickster Makes the World. The trickster defies definition; he is that ‘slippery figure’ that Plato chases in the Sophist, almost- always catching him but never really so: whenever you feel like you have a hold on him, he is no longer there, or he has changed shape: the trickster defies final definition of time, place and character (Ballinger 1985: 55). Sophists or tricksters live for the attention of the public, and they play with words and images, but they disregard the real nature of their own acts. 6 We will not be able here to enter the important discussion of whether trickster figures can be discussed as one universal ‘type’. It is evident that tricksters are widely different in various cultural and historical contexts; however, they are also sufficiently similar to allow for generalization, whether or not one adopts the Jungian position, seeing trickster as an archetype. Dimensions of Liminality 105 They think they are the originators of a new world, they have no sense of measure, and they equally disregard social effects. They cannot trust other humans, and the trust that people invest in them will only be used against those same people. In van Gennep’s short comment on Hitler in 1933, he had understood something very central here, which has since been too often forgotten: The defining feature of terror regimes is not order, system and repression – it is ambivalence and the constant obfuscation of categories, sacred reversals that turn things on their head. Tricksters are trained in upsetting the social order by reversing values, and via their rhetorical and theatrical skills. As Weber recognized, in moments of radical social or political change, in ‘out-of-the-ordinary moments’, we see the emergence of charismatic leadership, but what Weber failed to notice is that in such moments we also see the emergence of a whole series of other sinister figures. Concerning the role of leadership in liminal moments, it is certainly no coincidence that Turner kept coming back to the figure of the trickster as one of several (archetypical, as Jung (1972) claimed?) liminal figures, although he never subjected them to an in-depth analysis. In one of his last essays, Body, Brain and Culture, Turner even suggested that the ‘slippery’ tricksters are figures that move between the hemispheres of the brain (1988: 170), creating a real effect but erasing their own trace. And here one recurrent feature of the trickster figure must not be overlooked: while a breaker of taboos, tricksters often also attempt to perform a socially didactic function (Ballinger 1989: 15). In other words, the trickster mimics charisma (Horvath 2013: 9), and his magnetic powers must certainly not be underestimated, hollow as they are. The trickster represents the void; but as such he is also indestructible: there is nothing to take away. Nothingness never grows or diminishes; it makes other things grow and diminish, alchemically so. The analysis of the trickster as a particularly dangerous type of political leader that may emerge in liminal situations, as proposed by Horvath (1998), may well represent a breakthrough in our understanding of how liminal moments or periods may be carried in dangerous directions. Turner himself came close to saying something similar (1985: 230). Schismogenesis Gregory Bateson developed this concept (1958: 175) in his Cambridge doctoral dissertation, which broke both from structural functionalist and Marxist anthropology, earning the disapproval of Radcliffe-Brown and Malinowski. The word, just like ethos and eidos, central to Bateson’s book, is strongly Platonic in inspiration (for further discussion see Horvath and Thomassen, 2008). Bateson’s academic trajectory is in fact quite similar to that of Victor Turner. During his fieldwork among the Iatmul, Bateson became aware that the conceptual tools he had brought with him into the field were utterly inadequate. Bateson’s revolt was formulated in a most extraordinary piece of work, his 1936 Epilogue to Naven, the published version of his doctoral dissertation. This Epilogue combines, half a century before Bourdieu and the ‘reflexive turn’ in the social sciences, Liminality and the Modern 106 the conclusion of a piece of research with autobiographical reflections, creating – not surprisingly – a great deal of consternation. The Epilogue starts by Bateson revealing his complete failure to understand what was going on among the Iatmul, and the lack of any ‘guiding idea’ for his research (Bateson 1958 [1936]: 257–8). However, it soon becomes clear that Bateson’s ‘failure’ was not simply the result of his personal shortcomings as a researcher: it belonged to the ruling scientific paradigms. Bateson was astonishingly explicit here: ‘I could not (and still cannot) see that orthodox functional analysis was likely to lead anywhere’ (Bateson 1958: 257). Bateson was judged guilty of treason by his Cambridge environment and he would never get a job in an anthropology department. In Naven, Bateson makes a move similar to what Turner would later do: instead of attempting a ‘total’ or holistic description of an entire society, Bateson reads the Iatmul via a few central rituals. Closely observing the Naven ritual, characterized by very aggressive behaviour combined with mimicking, ridiculing and cross-dressing, Bateson came to the recognition that societies can be stuck for a long time in a state in which the previous unity was broken, and yet the schismatic components are forced to stay together, producing an unpleasant, violent, harrowing, truly miserable existence. Not surprisingly, Girard read Bateson’s work on the Iatmul with great interest, as it evidently confirmed some critical aspects of mimetic violence and imitation. However, in Naven Bateson’s aim was not simply to expose the flawed nature of functionalism or to denounce anthropological science as a hopeless ideal; far from it: he tried, hesitantly and in an honestly searching manner, to develop a different kind of conceptual vocabulary and epistemological footing for doing cultural analysis. It was as a part of this attempt that Bateson developed the concept of schismogenesis. In terms of definitions, Bateson (1958: 175) described schismogenesis as, ‘A process of differentiation in the norms of individual behavior resulting from cumulative interaction between individuals’. Just like van Gennep, Bateson sought to provide an empirically grounded analysis of differentiation processes, rather than assuming an original state of un-differentiation in ‘simple’ societies. While paying much attention to gender relations and ritualized mockery in his ethnographic analysis of the Iatmul, Bateson clearly indicated how schismogenetic processes could be at work in other spheres of social life. Right from the outset Bateson was moving the application of the concept beyond the specific cultural context. For Bateson schismogenesis could become part of any communication system or ‘communication relationship’ where individuals or groups interact. The behaviour of person X affects person Y, and the reaction of person Y to person X’s behaviour will then affect person X’s behaviour, which in turn will effect person Y, and so on, potentially leading to a ‘vicious circle’. Bateson’s schismogenesis theory is indeed about such circles. It is in the form of such circles that the ‘system’ is somehow ‘functioning’ although it may produce undesirable effects for everyone involved. That is why Bateson was not afraid to talk about pathologies in communication and in epistemology. Dimensions of Liminality 107 Bateson usefully distinguished between complementary and symmetrical schismogenesis. In the former, two ‘opposite’ types of behaviour reinforce each other in ‘opposite directions’: assertive versus submissive behaviour between two persons or two groups is the oft-quoted example here. In the latter, the ‘same’ behaviour will lead to more of the ‘same’ on the part of the other individual or group – a repetitive system of escalating competition: boasting leading to more boasting is the example invoked by Bateson himself (p. 177). Symmetrical relationships are those in which the two parties are equals, competitors, such as in sports, party politics or wars. Complementary relationships feature an unequal balance, such as dominance-submission (parent-child), or exhibitionism- spectatorship (performer-audience). Both types of schismogenesis can easily lead to extremities and become established as pathological states. There is every reason to bring together the insights of Gregory Bateson and Victor Turner and the comparative literature on the trickster. The process of schismogenesis can positively be produced by trickster-like figures. As Beebe expresses it, albeit in a different context, the trade of the trickster is to accomplish ‘an unsettling ambivalence, a splitting into two minds’ (1981: 38). This split, says Beebe, can occur ‘within a single individual, or between members of a large audience’ (ibid.). It is typical of the trickster, in art as in life ‘to split people into warring camps’ (ibid.). The trickster in many ways operates himself exactly the kind of double bind that Bateson so famously diagnosed as underlying schizophrenia. The victim of the double bind systematically receives contradictory injunctions or emotional messages on different levels of communication; he is caught in the communication game and cannot leave it. Drawing together the notions introduced so far, this means that schism and the splitting into two is a process that is particularly prone to unfolding in liminal moments, and that it can, under given circumstances, establish itself as a lasting form. This, it seems, can happen in two different ways: the first is when the ritual sequence is
